coredata.dc:title: Engineering a carbon dot-decorated fluorescent nanoplatform to promote heavy metal reutilization and photothermal evaporation with antibacterial activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fei
Liu, Sichen
Huo, Xiaokui
Liang, Guobiao
Yang, Fangyu
Zhang, Baojing
Feng, Lei
James, Tony D.
Li, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Solar steam generation is an efficient way to address global freshwater shortages. Heavy metals in wastewater pose serious environmental challenges and result in significant resource wastage. Developing an evaporator that integrates efficient water evaporation function, heavy metal (such as Fe) detection capability, and removal technology, with the aim of upgrading potentially discarded heavy metals into valuable materials, is undoubtedly an important technological challenge that urgently needs to be overcome. Herein, we report a fluorescent hydrogel grafted with lignin-based carbon dots (N-CDs) for Fe(III) ion recognition, self-assembled within a 3D tannic acid-based hydrogel network. The quantum yield of N-CDs is 5.9 % (λex = 300 nm, λem = 342 nm). The fluorescent hydrogel exhibited excellent sensitivity for Fe(III) ion detection within the range of 0 to 200 µM, with a detection limit of 5.4 × 10-4 M. Adsorption experiments confirmed that the hydrogel exhibited a high Fe(III) ion extraction capacity of over 239.2 mg g−1 at room temperature. Subsequently, the exhausted waste material was converted into a material suitable for solar steam generation. The upcycled hydrogel evaporators exhibited outstanding evaporation rates of 2.62 kg m-2h−1 under one sun irradiation and displayed superior antibacterial efficacy. These findings not only provide a strategy for controlling heavy metal ions but also offer a pathway to recycle hazardous waste for wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent hydrogel
Fe(III) ions
Solar steam generation
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of N-CDs, PTA-CDs and PTA-CDs/Fe(III)
Fluorescence properties of N-CDs and PTA-CDs
Investigation of solar steam generation efficiency

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work is supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant #: 2023MS146 ) and the Open Research Fund of the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering , Henan Normal University . ( 2020ZD01 and 2021YB07 ).

The present work is supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant #: 2023MS146) and the Open Research Fund of the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. (2020ZD01 and 2021YB07).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs and PTA-CDs/Fe(III) ions. (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: size distribution histogram).; (b) HRTEM image of CDs; (c) FTIR spectra of N-CDs; (d) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs; (e-f) Narrow scanning of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s in N-CDs; (g) SEM image of PTA-CDs/Fe(III) ions aerogel; (h, h1) Cross-sectional SEM images and EDS mapping of PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel; (i) FTIR spectra. (j-l) XPS survey spectrum at a wide scan, O 1 s, Fe 2p, N 1 s, and C 1 s for PTA-CDs aerogel and PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel.

Fluorescence properties of CDs and PTA-CDs. (a) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were recorded upon the addition of varying concentrations of Fe(III) (0–150 μM) with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. (b) The fluorescence spectra of PTA-CDs in aqueous solution were measured upon the addition of different concentrations of Fe(III) (0–200 μM), also at an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. (c) The fluorescence ratio of the fluorescent hydrogel PTA-CDs was analyzed in the presence of various metal ions at a concentration of 200 μM. (d) The influence of pH on the detection of Fe(III) was evaluated. (e) The impact of different contact times on the uptake capacity for Fe(III) was assessed (Inset: Fitting results of the pseudo-second-order model). (f) Shows the adsorption capacity of PTA-CDs with different temperature and not with different concentrations.

Performance of Solar Steam Generation. (a) Schematic representation of solar steam generation using PTA-CDs/Fe(III). (b) Temperature changes of PTA, PTA-CDs, and PTA-CDs/Fe(III) under one sun irradiation (100 mW cm−2). (c) Comparison of the evaporation rate and efficiency of the gel in different pH environments (pH = 2, pH = 13) under an irradiation of 100 mW cm−2 . (d) Time-dependent mass change of the gel under one sun irradiation. (e) Evaporation rate and energy efficiency of the PTA, PTA-CDs, and PTA-CDs/Fe(III). (f) Water cycle experiment demonstrating the reusability of the gel. (g) Correlation between irradiation intensities (sun) and evaporation rates for PTA-CDs/Fe(III) at various times. (h) Comparison of water evaporation rates and energy efficiencies with previously reported carbon-based or biomass-based materials under one sun irradiation.

Antimicrobial properties of PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel. Escherichia coli (red), Staphylococcus aureus (blue), Rhodotorula glutinis (black) and Candida albicans (green) were divided into control group, light group, PTA-CDs/Fe(III) group, PTA-CDs/Fe(III) and light group.
Biosafety investigation of condensed water. (a) Schematic diagram of biosafety investigation. (b) The curve of body weight change in male mice. (c) The curve of body weight change in female mice.
Schematic illustration of the application of PTA aerogel in the removal of heavy metal ions (Fe(III)) from metallurgical plant wastewater and its use in photothermal antibacterial treatment.

head.ce:title: Engineering a carbon dot-decorated fluorescent nanoplatform to promote heavy metal reutilization and photothermal evaporation with antibacterial activity

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar steam generation is an efficient way to address global freshwater shortages. Heavy metals in wastewater pose serious environmental challenges and result in significant resource wastage. Developing an evaporator that integrates efficient water evaporation function, heavy metal (such as Fe) detection capability, and removal technology, with the aim of upgrading potentially discarded heavy metals into valuable materials, is undoubtedly an important technological challenge that urgently needs to be overcome. Herein, we report a fluorescent hydrogel grafted with lignin-based carbon dots (N-CDs) for Fe(III) ion recognition, self-assembled within a 3D tannic acid-based hydrogel network. The quantum yield of N-CDs is 5.9 % (λex = 300 nm, λem = 342 nm). The fluorescent hydrogel exhibited excellent sensitivity for Fe(III) ion detection within the range of 0 to 200 µM, with a detection limit of 5.4 × 10-4 M. Adsorption experiments confirmed that the hydrogel exhibited a high Fe(III) ion extraction capacity of over 239.2 mg g−1 at room temperature. Subsequently, the exhausted waste material was converted into a material suitable for solar steam generation. The upcycled hydrogel evaporators exhibited outstanding evaporation rates of 2.62 kg m-2h−1 under one sun irradiation and displayed superior antibacterial efficacy. These findings not only provide a strategy for controlling heavy metal ions but also offer a pathway to recycle hazardous waste for wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth in global industrialization, the demand for heavy metal resources has increased dramatically [1,2]. While, industrial activities discharge millions of tons of heavy metal pollutants, which not only cause serious environmental problems and human health risks, but also result in the waste of heavy metal resources [3–5]. For example, iron ions (Fe(III) ions) are an important industrial raw material, however, excessive iron ions in wastewater can combine with other substances to form difficult-to-degrade precipitates, posing hazards to aquatic organisms, while such iron resources are difficult to recover [6,7]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for sensitive detection and control of heavy metal ions, such as Fe(III) ions, to enable resource utilization. Among the various sensing and purification technologies available, fluorescent hydrogels have attracted significant attention due to their low cost, ease of operation, and high efficiency. Among the diverse fluorescent sensing materials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained prominence as nanosensors for detecting heavy metal ions [8–11]. This is due to their excellent water solubility, non-toxicity, photostability, and ease of functionalization [12–16]. Thus, green CDs with high sensitivity and selectivity for Fe(III) ions can be used as sensing elements in the preparation of fluorescent hydrogels. Moreover, the zero-dimensional structure of nano CDs allows for their incorporation into a three-dimensional hydrogel network, enhancing the number of chelating sites and facilitating the capture of Fe(III) ions. This integration significantly improves the sensitivity and adsorption capacity of the material.

Recent studies have revealed that tannic acid (TA) as a traditional Chinese medicinal product, not only rapidly forms complexes with iron ions through specific phenolic hydroxyl groups, but also gives rise to organic-metal complexes with robust absorption with high photothermal conversion efficiency, which could further promote iron reutilization. Therefore, a TA-based hydrogel can serve as a scaffold, while CDs can function as fluorescence units in the creation of a fluorescent hydrogel. This hydrogel would be capable of detecting and removing Fe(III) ions with a low detection limit and high adsorption capacity. These fluorescent nanoplatforms have significant potential for treating heavy metal-polluted water. However, the increasing demand for water treatment has led to the rapid accumulation of exhausted adsorbents due to their limited lifespan. Many polymeric adsorbents end up in landfills, which is wasteful, unsustainable, and costly. To mitigate environmental impact and promote sustainable development, it is crucial to find efficient ways to upcycle exhausted materials into valuable products rather than contributing to already overwhelmed landfills [17]. This challenge has driven the development of methods to repurpose such hazardous materials for freshwater production.

One promising approach is the use of photothermal evaporation combined with antibacterial properties. Solar energy has been extensively studied due to its abundant, renewable, and environmentally friendly nature [18,19]. Recent studies have shown that the phenolic hydroxyl group in tannic acid achieves efficient capture and selective chelation of Fe(III) [20,21]. This interaction results in broader and stronger light absorption. The Fe(III)/TA complexes exhibit exceptional photothermal efficiency, approximately 40 %, due to their formation of organo-metal complexes that possess robust absorption properties and high photothermal conversion efficiency [22,23]. The three-dimensional aerogel nanostructure enhances the efficiency of photothermal and solar energy conversion by minimizing heat loss through localized heating at the air–water interface during the transfer process [24,25]. Consequently, these materials can effectively absorb solar radiation and generate heat for water evaporation and antibacterial purposes. This approach offers significant benefits, both for the fundamental reuse of hazardous waste and for practical applications in solar-driven water purification technologies.

Within this research, we present a hydrogel (PTA) synthesized by cross-linking polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with tannic acid (TA), incorporating nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) into the functionalized PTA-based hydrogel via hydrogen bonding for the detection and removal of Fe(III) ions from wastewater (Scheme 1 ). The N-CDs exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Fe(III) ion detection due to specific coordination interactions between Fe(III) ion and −N [26–28]. Significantly, the N-CDs were employed as the fluorescent component of the hydrogel sensor to facilitate rapid Fe(III) ions detection, and the mechanisms behind the quenching phenomenon and adsorption capability were examined. Additionally, the exhausted materials were repurposed for photothermal evaporation and antibacterial applications to generate freshwater. This strategy offers significant potential for creating multifunctional materials that not only purify heavy metal ions but also upcycle hazardous waste into valuable resources.

CDs are synthesized via hydrothermal method. Delignified lignin (0.1 g) and O-phenylenediamine (0.3 g) were added to DI water (10 mL) with stirring, and sonication for 30 min to produce a homogeneous solution. Finally, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h. The autoclave was then cooled to room temperature. The CD solution is dialyzed (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the purified brown solution was dried using a freeze dryer to obtain the CD powder.

2.0 g PVA was added to deionized water (10 mL) and stirred in water bath at 90 ℃ for 20 min. After cooling to 20 ℃, 120 μL glutaraldehyde (25 % wt) was added and stirred to form solution A. Tannic acid (0.265 g) and 80 μL hydrochloric acid were added into deionized water (10 mL) and stirred for 5 min to form solution B. After that, solution B was added to solution A and stirred (5 min), and then the liquid was ultrasonic (20 min) to remove the bubbles from the solution and leave it at room temperature (24 ℃) for 4 h to form hydrogel (PTA). Finally, the PTA hydrogel underwent immersion in a solution of CDs (1 g/L). After 24 h, it was washed three times with DI water to obtain the fluorescent hydrogel PTA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs, PTA-CDs and PTA-CDs/Fe(III)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using delignified lignin as the carbon source and O-phenylenediamine for surface passivation [29]. The structural and morphological characterization of N-CDs was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1 a and 1b, TEM imaging depicts well-dispersed N-CDs with an average diameter of 4.76 ± 0.4 nm. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of N-CDs exhibited prominent peaks corresponding to –OH stretching bands at 3373 cm−1, N-H stretching vibrations at 1594 cm−1, and C-N group stretching vibration at 1039 cm−1 (Fig. 1c). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of N-CDs revealed the presence of C, O and N elements, corroborating the incorporation of O-phenylenediamine (Fig. 1d). To confirm whether N-CDs have carbon nanostructure characteristics similar to graphene, we conducted X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis and Raman spectroscopic evaluation. The XRD pattern shows a significant peak at approximately 21°, which reveals that the internal arrangement of carbon atoms has an amorphous structure similar to that of graphene carbon nanomaterials (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the Raman spectra of N-CDs (Fig. S2) exhibit a D band at 1356 cm -1 and a G band at 1599 cm−1. These two spectral bands correspond to the disordered (or defective) structure and graphitized structure in carbon materials, further confirming the structural similarity between N-CDs and graphene. A comparison between pure polyvinyl alcohol (PTA) and PTA-CDs showed an XRD peak at 31°, confirming successful functionalization of PTA with N-CDs. The high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra for C 1 s and O 1 s are depicted in Fig. 1e. The C 1 s spectrum exhibited three distinct peaks at 287.9, 286.1, and 284.6 eV, corresponding to N-C = O, C-N, and C-C functionalities, respectively [30]. In the O 1 s XPS spectrum, peaks at 533.4 eV and 531.8 eV indicate the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups on the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), corresponding to C-O, C=O bonds. The N 1 s spectrum reveals peaks at 400.5 eV, 399.4 eV, and 398.7 eV, which are attributed to –NH2, –NH-/–NH2, and C-N groups, respectively [31]. This data support the interaction between the –NH2 group and the carboxyl groups in delignified lignin (Fig. 1f). A fluorescent hydrogel was subsequently synthesized through intermolecular interactions between the hydrogel matrix and N-CDs. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and tannic acid (TA) serve as the structural framework, and glutaraldehyde (GA) serves as the crosslinking agent, forming a double network structure [32]. Additionally, N-CDs were employed as optical sensors for detecting Fe(III) ions. The successful integration of N-CDs into the PTA aerogel was confirmed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). SEM imaging illustrated the porous nature of the PTA-CDs gel, which provides numerous adsorption sites for metal ion interaction (Fig. 1g, h). EDS mapping showed a uniform distribution of C, O, and N elements within the PTA-CDs gel, corroborating the XPS findings (Fig. S3). Before and after adsorption, the tensile vibration peaks of –OH (3294 cm−1), –NH (1432 cm−1) and C-N (1077 cm−1) decreased, resulting in redshift. This is due to the adsorption of Fe(III) on the gel and the chelation of the phenol hydroxyl group (Fig. 1i). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the PTA aerogel revealed the presence of C, O, and N elements (Fig. 1j). The O 1 s peak at 532.6 eV was associated with carboxyl groups, facilitating metal ion adsorption. The N 1 s spectrum exhibited components at 400.6 eV for –NH2, 398.7 eV for –NH2/–NH-, and 398.1 eV for C-N, which contribute to the adsorption properties (Fig. 1k). In the C 1 s spectrum, peaks at 286.6, 285.2, and 283.7 eV corresponded to C-O/C = O, C-N, and C-C bonds, reflecting the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups within the PTA gel (Fig. 1l). These results collectively confirm that the PTA aerogel incorporated N-CDs effectively, rendering it suitable for the sensing and removal of Fe(III) ions. In addition, the swelling of PTA-CDs hydrogel was measured to investigate the water retention properties. In Fig. S4, it can be found that the swelling ratio increased and reached equilibrium after about 5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of N-CDs and PTA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we studied the fluorescence properties of N-CDs and PTA-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs exhibited a UV–visible absorption band at 300 nm and a fluorescence peak at 342 nm (Fig. S5a). A significant spectral overlap was observed between the absorbance spectrum of Fe(III) ions and the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs, indicating the involvement of the inner filter effect (IFE) in the fluorescence quenching mechanism (Fig. S5b). Furthermore, these N-CDs exhibited blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, a robust linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity ratio of N-CDs and Fe(III) ions (0–200 × 10-6 M), yielding an excellent correlation coefficient of 0.9979. Stern-Volmer analysis yielded a quenching constant (KSV) value of 0.008. Furthermore, the PTA-CDs showed similar fluorescence response towards Fe(III) ions. The detection limit of PTA-CDs was determined to be 5.4 × 10-4 M using the 3δ/k criterion (where δ denotes the standard deviation of the blank N-CDs solution, and k denotes the slope of the linear plot) [33], indicating good sensitivity toward Fe(III) ions (Fig. 2b) [34]. Furthermore, the selectivity of the fluorescent hydrogel PTA-CDs toward various metal ions was assessed (Fig. 2c). Superior selectivity toward Fe(III) ions over others was observed. Afterwards, we also investigated the fluorescence detection effect of trivalent iron with different concentrations of carbon dots (Fig. S6), and found that the detection effect was best when the carbon dot concentration was 1 mg/mL. The influence of different pH (Fig. S7a) and time (Fig. S7b) on the fluorescence of PTA-CDs hydrogels have also been carried out. The results showed that the fluorescence properties of the hydrogel could reach a stable state in about 10 min. In addition, it was found that different pH had subtle changes on the fluorescence properties of hydrogels [35].

Potentially interfering ions, including Hg(II), Al(III), Cu(II), Cd(II), Cr(III), Mg(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Co(II) and K(I) were observed. Subsequently, the fluorescence performance of PTA-CDs with different metal ions at the same concentrations were investigated (Fig. S8-S9).

To further investigate the adsorption capabilities of PTA and PTA-CDs toward trivalent iron, we conducted comparative adsorption experiments and found that PTA-CDs exhibited superior adsorption efficiency toward Fe(III) ions (Fig. S10). In addition, we also carried out four cyclic adsorption experiments on PTA-CDs gel, and found that the gel reached the saturation state of cyclic adsorption after three times (Fig. S11). This selective fluorescence quenching of PTA-CDs by Fe(III) ions can be attributed to the strong electron-capturing ability of Fe(III) ions, leading to greater electron transfer from N-CDs and resulting in substantial fluorescence quenching [36]. These findings confirm the excellent selectivity of PTA-CDs toward Fe(III) ions. Subsequently, the adsorption properties of the PTA-CDs aerogel were systematically investigated. Initially, the impact of pH on Fe(III) ions absorption capacity was examined (Fig. 2d). Notably, at lower pH levels, the adsorption capacity was notably enhanced and remained effective until pH reached approximately 3. When pH ＞ 3, Fe(III) ions precipitation the occurred due to the influence of the solubility product constant (Ksp). At pH values ≤ 3, protonation of amino groups likely intensified electrostatic interactions with negatively charged HFeO4- ions. Therefore, pH = 3 was deemed appropriate for subsequent analyses. Further scrutiny of the system involved a kinetic study, revealing that equilibrium was achieved by the PTA aerogel after approximately 2 h (Fig. 2e).

Analysis using a pseudo-second-order model displayed superior fitting with a high linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.998), in contrast to the less satisfactory fit obtained with a pseudo-first-order model (Fig. S12). This dominance of the pseudo-second-order model indicates that chemical adsorption is the primary mechanism for Fe(III) ions capture, with boundary layer resistance not serving as the rate-limiting step. To further investigate the adsorption properties, the effect of temperature on the PTA aerogel was examined. As shown in Fig. 2f, the adsorption capacity increased as the temperature rose from 293 to 333 K. Thermodynamic parameters were subsequently calculated to elucidate the adsorption process, as detailed in Table S1. The positive ΔH value confirmed the endothermic nature of Fe(III) ions removal, indicating that the adsorption process is driven by entropy. Moreover, the overall negative ΔG value signifies the spontaneity of the Fe(III) ions adsorption process.

As the temperature increases, more negative ΔG values were observed, indicating enhanced adsorption at elevated temperatures, which has been experimentally confirmed. The range of ΔG values suggests a mixed mechanism for Fe(III) ions adsorption. It is plausible that the porous structure and various functional groups of the PTA-CDs aerogel contribute to the removal of Fe(III) ions during the adsorption process. The intraparticle diffusion model was applied to assess the adsorption process of Fe(III) ions by the PTA-CDs aerogel (Fig. S13). The adsorption process can be divided into three stages. The initial stage is characterized by rapid adsorption, likely dominated by membrane diffusion. The subsequent stage shows a gradual adsorption, which may involve both membrane diffusion and intragranular diffusion. The final equilibrium phase indicates saturation of adsorption sites on the PTA-CDs aerogel. The results suggest that factors other than intraparticle diffusion play a role in the adsorption process (Table S2). Furthermore, an isotherm model was employed to elucidate the interaction between the adsorbate and the adsorbents. Then, the adsorption capacities of different materials for Fe(III) were compared. PTA-CDs shows better detection and removal capabilities over other reported adsorbents. (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of solar steam generation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The complex formed by the chelation of tannic acid with ferric ion exhibits enhanced light absorption capability within the visible spectrum. This enhancement stems from both the structural characteristics of the complex and the aromatic ring structures inherent in tannic acid, enabling effective absorption of photon energy and thereby increasing the material's light absorption [37]. In addition, the absorption spectra of PTA, PTA-CDs, and PTA-CDs/Fe(III) were examined. It was observed that there were significant absorption band in the broad range of 200 to 700 nm (Fig. S14), which showed excellent light absorption ability. The incorporation of CDs and Fe(III) helps with the improvement of light absorption [38,39]. This efficiency enhancement arises from the ability of Fe(III) ions within the complex to promote charge separation and transfer, thereby increasing the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and enhancing photothermal conversion efficiency [40]. PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel were evaluated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) under nitrogen atmosphere at room temperature to 600 ℃ (Fig. S15a) and constant temperature of 58 ℃ (Fig. S15b). During the process from RT to 128℃, due to the loss of water, the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel have a slight quality degradation (not more than 5 %). PTA-CDs/Fe(III) have their first degradation at 211.2℃, and the maximum degradation temperature is 414.9℃ (loss of ca. 62 %). At the end of the analysis, the remaining PTA-CDs/Fe(III) was about 12 %. At a constant temperature of 58℃ for one hour, the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel has a slight mass degradation (no more than 3 %), confirming good thermal stability. Therefore, tannic acid-Fe(III) ions complexes exhibit higher thermal stability, maintaining stable photothermal performance even under high-temperature conditions. This stability is attributed to the structural stability of the complex and the strong chemical interactions between tannic acid and ferric ions [41]. The limited lifespan of fluorescent hydrogels, coupled with the increasing demand for water treatment, has led to the rapid accumulation of spent absorbents, thereby exacerbating environmental pollution. In addition, the shortage of heavy metal resources has also become an important problem. Hence, to foster sustainable development and mitigate environmental degradation, there is an urgent need for an efficient approach to repurpose exhausted materials into valuable products. One promising avenue for repurposing these materials involves leveraging solar thermal technology to achieve synergistic antibacterial photothermal water evaporation [42]. The PTA-CDs exhibit exceptional efficacy not only in the removal of Fe(III) ions but also in their potential application in solar water evaporation. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the color transition of PTA to black upon adsorption of Fe(III) ions is evident. This transformation results in the formation of a black PTA-CDs/Fe(III) composite, functioning as a “solar absorber” that efficiently converts light into heat. Consequently, this material holds promise for utilizing solar radiation to generate heat for seawater desalination. As shown in Fig. S16, steam generation by PTA-CDs/Fe(III) declined slightly with increasing salt concentrations, which indicates that the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) displays potential for applications in solar desalination.

The temperature changes of PTA, PTA-CDs, PTA-CDs/Fe(III) are measured by irradiation in a single sunlight with an infrared camera (Fig. S17). The surface temperatures of PTA, PTA-CDs and PTA-CDs /Fe (III) rapidly increased to 42.1℃, 45.2℃ and 56.4℃ within 20 min under 1 Sun, and then reached a steady state (Fig. 3b). After adding CDs, the photothermal effect was utilized to increase the surface temperature of PTA. When PTA-CDs adsorbed Fe(III) ions, the phenolic hydroxyl and ferric ions quickly complexed and the gel turned black, significantly enhancing the light absorption capacity and photothermal conversion efficiency. Subsequent to this, water evaporation tests were conducted on PTA-CDs/Fe(III) in various aqueous environments, revealing significant results. Particularly, in an acidic environment, the evaporation rate of the aerogel reached its zenith at 3.02 kg m-2h−1 (Fig. 3c). It is worth noting that PTA-CDs/Fe(III) exhibited superior performance compared to PTA-CDs, which is due to the enhanced optical properties of black PTA-CDs/Fe(III) compared to light yellow PTA-CDs. The presence of Fe(III) ions in PTA-CDs enhances the photothermal conversion efficiency, underscoring its remarkable thermal localization effect, which promotes the generation of solar steam. Subsequently, the vapor generation efficiency of both PTA-CDs and PTA-CDs/Fe(III) was assessed by monitoring the change in water evaporation rate with irradiation time under single solar irradiation conditions. Notably, PTA-CDs/Fe(III) exhibited superior performance compared to PTA-CDs, attributed to the enhanced optical properties of black PTA-CDs/Fe(III) in contrast to light yellow PTA-CDs. The enhanced conversion of light to heat facilitated by PTA-CDs/Fe(III) highlights its outstanding heat localization effect, which promotes efficient solar steam generation.

Subsequently, the steam generation efficiency of PTA, PTA-CDs and PTA-CDs/Fe(III) was evaluated by monitoring the water evaporation rate as a function of irradiation time under one sun illumination conditions. As shown in Fig. 3d, the water evaporation rate of PTA-CDs/Fe (III) reaches 2.62 kg m-2h−1, which is better than the 0.74 kg m-2h−1 of PTA-CDs and 0.52 kg m-2h−1 of PTA. The corresponding energy efficiency of PTA-CDs /Fe(III) under a single solar irradiation was 89.8 %, higher than that of PTA (35.3 %) and PTA-CDs (50.3 %), underscoring its potential as a solar evaporator (Fig. 3e). Moreover, we assessed the water evaporation capacity of PTA-CDs/Fe(III) under varying intensities of incident sunlight (Fig. S18). The water evaporation rates under different optical strengths (0.5–––2.0 sun) were recorded as 1.76, 2.62, 3.30 and 4.31 kg m-2h−1, respectively, confirming an increase in evaporation rate with the rise in solar intensity. Furthermore, the evaporation enthalpy of pure water and water within PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel was measured under dark conditions to explore the impact of PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel on water evaporation (Fig. S19). The equivalent evaporation enthalpy of PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel was significantly lower than that of pure water, suggesting a reduction in the energy demand for water evaporation facilitated by the aerogel. The abundant presence of –OH and –COOH groups on the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel effectively enhances its hydration ability within the hydrogel network, activating water molecules and thereby reducing the energy demand for water evaporation. Furthermore, the trend of the obtained equivalent evaporation enthalpy of water within the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel, as determined experimentally, aligns well with the results obtained from differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). However, the enthalpy values calculated by DSC are higher than those determined through evaporation experiments, attributed to the complete and slight dehydration processes exhibited by DSC and evaporation tests, respectively.

The data presented in Fig. 3f support the contention that the water evaporation rate for the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel remained stable over ten cycles, indicating significant photothermal stability in water. Contact angle results confirmed that the PVA-CDs/Fe(III) was hydrophilic, since a water droplet permeated into the PVA-CDs/Fe(III) within 1.8 s. In contrast, when a drop of water falls on the surface of PVA-CDs, it remains on the surface for at least 8.7 s (Fig. S20). This stability is attributed to the hydrophilic properties of the aerogel and the presence of porous channels. Additionally, the surfaces of the aerogel can absorb energy from the environment and promote water evaporation. However, slight fluctuations in water evaporation during the cyclic test were observed, potentially due to experimental conditions or changes in the pore structure of the materials. Nevertheless, the material maintained reasonable photothermal evaporation. Considering these findings collectively, we propose that the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel holds promise as a solar evaporator.

Moreover, a laboratory-scale desalination apparatus was employed for water purification under real-world outdoor conditions (Fig. 3g). An outdoor experiment for solar steam generation was conducted to evaluate its performance under natural sunlight conditions (Fig. 3h), spanning from 9:30 am to 5:30 pm, with an average solar heat flux of approximately 92.4 mW cm−2. This experiment resulted in an evaporation rate of 8.21 kg m-2h−1 (Baoding, China, Longitude: 114.82 E, Latitude: 37.21 N, temperature around 26 °C, 58 % humidity, and level 5 westerly wind with speeds ranging from 5.80 to 10.7 m s−1) (Fig. S21). The notably high evaporation rate of 8.21 kg m-2h−1 was attributed to changes in the incident angle of outdoor sunlight. Both the surface and sides of the evaporator functioned as heated areas for vapor generation during sunlight exposure. Benefitting from faster water transportation, effective light-harvesting and precise regulation of wettability, the water evaporation rate and thermal conversion efficiency of the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) aerogel surpassed those of most reported solar evaporators (Fig. 3h) [43–53].

Tannic acid is widely recognized in the medical field as a potent antioxidant with antibacterial and antifungal properties, effectively inhibiting microbial growth [54]. Its hydroxyl groups form complexes with Fe(III) ions, a chemical interaction that may alter the microbial growth environment, influencing internal pH values and ion concentrations, thereby achieving microbial growth inhibition [37]. Consequently, to further investigate the antibacterial effects of aerogels, relevant antibacterial tests were conducted (Fig. 4 ). Four kinds of microorganisms in logarithmic growth stage (Escherichia coli (red), Staphylococcus aureus (blue), Rhodotorula glutinis (black) and Candida albicans (green)) were divided into control group, light group, PTA-CDs/Fe(III) group, PTA-CDs/Fe(III) with light group. The light conditions involved simulated sunlight of 100 mW cm−2 for 15 min. After the bacteria solution was treated with PTA-CDs/Fe(III) material under 1 sun illumination, it is clearly indicated that no colonies were found in the collected condensed water on the solid medium, indicating excellent removal of bacteria in the evaporation system.

The presence of large amounts of heavy metals in wastewater poses serious challenge to biosafety. Inspired by the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) composite’s ability to enrich Fe(III) ions in wastewater and purify it, the collected condensed water was used to investigate the biosafety in mice (Fig. 5 a). The body weight changes of mice are a key factor to assess the toxicity of condensed water in the mice. In Fig. 5b and 5c, there were obvious deceases in body weight for both of male and female C57BL/6 mice after intragastric administration of Fe(III) ion solution. The mice were given stroke-physiological saline solution (SPSS) or condensed water in the same way of administration for 7 days, exhibited relatively constant body weight. These results suggest that the condensed water has no effect on the body weight and is safe for organisms, which confirms that the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) composite exhibits the capability to purify wastewater and possesses promising potential for practical applications.

We describe a method for introducing N-CDs into PTA to detect and enrich Fe(III) ions from wastewater. Within a linear range of 0 to 200 μM, PTA-CDs exhibited a rapid response to Fe(III) ions. Adsorption experiments confirmed the excellent Fe(III) ion extraction capacity of PTA-CDs, with an extraction capacity exceeding 239.2 mg g−1. Furthermore, we found that the saturated adsorbent (PTA-CDs/Fe(III)) material displayed a high solar steam generation efficiency under one sun irradiation, approximately 2.62 kg m-2h−1. As such, our material exhibits the capability to adsorb solar radiation and harness energy for the purification of pollutants or seawater, showcasing promising potential for practical applications. Concurrently, the PTA-CDs/Fe(III) composite offers a novel approach to tackling heavy metal resource shortages and the repurposing hazardous waste, thereby contributing to the mitigation of environmental pollution and the enrichment of heavy metal resources. Additionally, we hope that our research can promote the preparation of multifunctional materials suitable for purifying heavy metal ions in polluted water, and these materials can be upgraded to value-added products after being exhausted.

Fei Yan: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Sichen Liu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Xiaokui Huo: Resources, Investigation. Guobiao Liang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Fangyu Yang: Methodology, Investigation. Baojing Zhang: Supervision. Lei Feng: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Tony D. James: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Meng Li: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work is supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant #: 2023MS146) and the Open Research Fund of the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. (2020ZD01 and 2021YB07).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.158890.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
